cuisine: Filipino — Ilocano regional dish, northern Luzon; widely adopted nationwide
food_subgroup: Mixed vegetable braised dish with fermented condiment and pork (eggplant, bitter gourd, squash, long beans, okra, tomato, pork belly, bagoong alamang)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for pinakbet. Closest parent: FOODON:03315595 (vegetable dish).
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed cooked vegetable dish with meat and fermented seasoning
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for pinakbet. Closest parent category: A0176 (Mixed cooked vegetable dish with meat and fermented seasoning).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for pinakbet or equivalent Filipino mixed vegetable stew identified in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pinakbet weighs approximately 250g, comprising roughly 220g of mixed vegetables (eggplant, bitter gourd, squash, long beans, okra, tomato), 30g of pork belly, and 10g of bagoong alamang (fermented shrimp paste).

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines) — Pinakbet and component vegetables; ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables for bitter gourd reference data
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Filipino mixed vegetable dishes and individual component vegetables (eggplant, bitter gourd, squash, long beans, okra, tomato) combined with pork belly and bagoong alamang reference values. Sodium value is dominated by bagoong alamang contribution (~680mg/100g composite). ICMR-NIN data used for bitter gourd clinical phytonutrient reference. Values represent a weighted composite for the 250g standard plate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Vegetable Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for bitter gourd and non-starchy vegetable composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pinakbet exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) non-starchy mixed vegetable dishes with GI 35–45 in the Sydney GI Database; (2) bitter gourd (ampalaya) documented hypoglycaemic properties via charantin and polypeptide-p, which may further reduce effective GI; (3) low net carbohydrate content (6g/100g) dominated by dietary fibre. GL per 250g serving = (38 × 15g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.7 (LOW). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium from bagoong alamang (fermented shrimp paste): ~1,700mg per 250g plate (680mg/100g), exceeding 70% of the 2,300mg daily sodium limit in a single serving.
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit or substitute bagoong alamang — using 5g instead of 10g per plate reduces sodium by approximately 400–500mg; the mixed vegetables themselves (potassium ~700mg/plate, high fibre, low calorie) are beneficial for blood pressure, but the fermented shrimp paste is the dominant sodium source and must be minimised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic load (~5.7 per plate), high dietary fibre (6.3g/plate), and bitter gourd bioactives (charantin, polypeptide-p) with documented modest anti-hyperglycaemic activity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pinakbet is one of the most suitable Filipino dishes for Type 2 Diabetes management: GL ~6 (LOW), 6.3g fibre per plate supports post-meal glycaemic control, and bitter gourd provides charantin and polypeptide-p which may modestly reduce post-prandial blood glucose; sodium management remains important if the patient also has hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content (~1,700mg/plate) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat from pork belly is modest (3g/plate); the vegetable components provide cardioprotective fibre, potassium, vitamin C, and beta-carotene.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, pinakbet's vegetable base (high fibre, potassium, antioxidants) is cardioprotective, but the high sodium from bagoong alamang poses a significant risk for blood pressure and fluid retention — advise reducing bagoong to a minimum and pairing with a low-sodium meal for the rest of the day; saturated fat from 30g pork belly (~3g/plate) is not clinically concerning at this portion size.

g6pd_deficiency.key_factor: Bitter gourd (ampalaya) contains vicine and other oxidant compounds that may trigger haemolytic episodes in G6PD-deficient individuals.
g6pd_deficiency.guidance: Individuals with G6PD deficiency should exercise caution with pinakbet due to the bitter gourd content; the risk is dose-dependent and a small amount (as in a standard plate) may be tolerable for some, but avoidance is the safest recommendation pending individual clinical assessment.

halal_dietary_requirement.key_factor: Contains pork belly and bagoong alamang (fermented shrimp paste) — both are non-Halal ingredients.
halal_dietary_requirement.guidance: Pinakbet is NOT Halal: it contains pork belly and bagoong alamang (fermented shrimp paste from shellfish); Muslim patients should avoid this dish unless prepared with Halal-certified substitutes (e.g., chicken or beef instead of pork, and Halal-certified fermented fish paste instead of shrimp paste).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pinakbet (also spelled pakbet) is a traditional Filipino braised vegetable stew originating from the Ilocano people of the Ilocos region in northern Luzon, Philippines. The name derives from the Ilocano word 'pinakebbet' meaning 'shrivelled' or 'wilted', describing the technique of simmering vegetables until slightly wilted but still intact. A standard 250g plate contains approximately 220g of mixed vegetables — eggplant (talong), bitter gourd (ampalaya), squash (kalabasa), long beans (sitaw), okra, and tomato — along with 30g of pork belly and 10g of bagoong alamang (fermented shrimp paste). The dish is braised rather than stir-fried, producing a moist, savoury stew with a slightly bitter note from the ampalaya and a deeply umami, salty flavour from the bagoong. Per 250g plate: approximately 180 kcal, 12g protein, 8.8g fat, 15g carbohydrate, 6.3g dietary fibre, and ~1,700mg sodium. The sodium is almost entirely derived from the bagoong alamang, not the vegetables. Glycemic index ~38 (LOW), glycemic load ~5.7 (LOW), making it one of the most diabetes-friendly Filipino dishes. Bitter gourd contains charantin and polypeptide-p, bioactives with documented modest anti-hyperglycaemic properties. The dish is rich in Vitamin C (~45mg/plate), beta-carotene (~238mcg RAE/plate), potassium (~700mg/plate), and dietary fibre. Clinically, pinakbet is excellent for Type 2 Diabetes and weight management but poses a HIGH sodium concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients due to bagoong. It is NOT Halal (pork + shellfish). G6PD-deficient individuals should exercise caution due to bitter gourd content. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table (Philippines). Confidence: HIGH.
meal_context: Filipino lunch or dinner side dish

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table (Philippines), an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN for bitter gourd clinical data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy mixed vegetable dishes, with documented basis. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from the approved source composite.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for pinakbet as a composite dish not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for a braised vegetable and pork belly dish with no hydrogenated fats). All core macros (protein_g 4.8, fat_g 3.5, carbohydrate_g 6.0) are non-zero and well-supported. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for pinakbet exists in the University of Sydney GI Database — estimation basis documented in gi_source. Sodium of 680mg/100g (1,700mg/plate) is dominated by bagoong alamang contribution; the vegetable base alone would contribute approximately 80–120mg sodium/100g. Bitter gourd (ampalaya) caution for G6PD-deficient individuals documented in health_context. This dish is NOT Halal due to pork belly and shellfish-derived bagoong alamang. Nightshade sensitivity note: eggplant and tomato are present — individuals with nightshade sensitivity or rheumatoid arthritis who avoid nightshades should be aware. Ilocano variant uses bagoong isda (fermented fish paste) instead of bagoong alamang; Tagalog variant uses bagoong alamang — this record represents the Tagalog/nationwide variant with bagoong alamang. Q8 aliases consistency self-check (iteration 3): all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim in flat aliases array — english: [Pinakbet, pakbet, pinakbet Tagalog, pakbet Tagalog, Filipino mixed vegetable stew, Filipino vegetable medley with shrimp paste, Ilocano vegetables, Filipino vegetable stew with bagoong] — all present; chinese: [菲律宾什锦蔬菜炖虾酱, 菲律賓什錦蔬菜燉蝦醬] — all present; malay: [Rebusan sayur Filipina dengan pes udang] — present; tamil: [பிலிப்பைன்ஸ் காய்கறி குழம்பு இறால் விழுதுடன்] — present; hindi: [फिलिपिनो सब्जी स्टू झींगा पेस्ट के साथ] — present; romanized: [pinakbet Ilocano, Pinakbet / Pakbet] — both present. Consistency confirmed.
